front: Poem: opens 'April is the cruellest month'; paraphrases Dante with 'I had not thought death had undone so many'
Poem: Section III repeats 'burning burning burning burning'; partly narrated by Tiresias; Phlebas the Phoenician appears 'a fortnight dead'
Poem: speaker asks 'Do I dare / Disturb the universe?' and repeats 'there will be time' among 'a hundred visions and revisions'
back: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock (T. S. Eliot)
work: The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock

front: Author: 'Tradition and the Individual Talent' argues for 'continual extinction of personality'; compares poet's mind to platinum in a chemical reaction
Author: coined 'objective correlative' in 'Hamlet and His Problems'; called Hamlet an 'artistic failure' and the Mona Lisa of literature
Author: 'The Metaphysical Poets' introduced 'dissociation of sensibility' and revived interest in Abraham Cowley; collected in The Sacred Wood
Poem: opens 'Because I do not hope to turn again' from Cavalcanti; a conversion poem to Anglicanism
Poem: travelers are 'no longer at ease here, in the old dispensation'; first lines taken from a Nativity sermon by Lancelot Andrewes
back: Journey of the Magi (T. S. Eliot)

front: Play: four knights loyal to Henry II kill Thomas Becket in Canterbury
back: Murder in the Cathedral (T. S. Eliot)

front: Poem: 'Burnt Norton' opens 'time present and time past / are both perhaps present in time future'; features an empty pool in a rose garden
Poem: 'The Dry Salvages' evokes the Mississippi as a 'strong brown god,' drawing on the poet's St. Louis upbringing
